vulnerability.title: Restrict Exposed Kernel Pointer Addresses Access
vulnerability.description: To set the runtime status of the kernel.kptr_restrict kernel parameter, run the following command: $ sudo sysctl -w kernel.kptr_restrict= To make sure that the setting is persistent, add the following line to a file in the directory /etc/sysctl.d: kernel.kptr_restrict =
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then # Comment out any occurrences of kernel.kptr_restrict from /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf files for f in /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf /run/sysctl.d/*.conf /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d/*.conf; do # skip systemd-sysctl symlink (/etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf -> /etc/sysctl.conf) if [[ "$(readlink -f "$f")" == "/etc/sysctl.conf" ]]; then continue; fi matching_list=$(grep -P '^(?!#).*[\s]*kernel.kptr_restrict.*$' $f | uniq ) if ! test -z "$matching_list"; then while IFS= read -r entry; do escaped_entry=$(sed -e 's|/|\\/|g' <<< "$entry") # comment out "kernel.kptr_restrict" matches to preserve user data sed -i --follow-symlinks "s/^${escaped_entry}$/# &/g" $f done <<< "$matching_list" fi done # # Set sysctl config file which to sa

triage_decision.reason: The remediation only edits or adds a sysctl entry for kernel.kptr_restrict and applies the value at runtime. This is a standard STIG sysctl hardening step that does not affect authentication, networking, or filesystem layout, and does not require a reboot. The change is low risk and reversible.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: May affect debugging tools that rely on kernel pointer visibility; Potential incompatibility with custom kernel modules that expect unrestricted pointers

llm_verdict.message: The kernel.kptr_restrict setting has been correctly remedied. It is now set to 1 at runtime and persisted through a dedicated sysctl file, with any prior conflicting entries commented out. This resolves the OpenSCAP finding.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan correctly addresses the vulnerability by ensuring the kernel.kptr_restrict setting is applied at runtime and persisted via a dedicated sysctl file. Minor refinements could improve robustness and idempotency.
concerns: The sed expression may not match all possible whitespace variations or comments with trailing characters, potentially leaving some un-commented entries.
The plan does not reload the full sysctl configuration after creating the new file, relying solely on the immediate sysctl -w command.
suggested_improvements: Use a more flexible regex or a tool like 'awk' to comment out any non‑commented kernel.kptr_restrict lines regardless of spacing.
After writing /etc/sysctl.d/60-kernel-kptr_restrict.conf, run 'sysctl --system' to reload all sysctl settings and ensure consistency.
Verify the applied value with 'sysctl kernel.kptr_restrict' and report success/failure.
Make the file creation idempotent by checking for existing /etc/sysctl.d/60-kernel-kptr_restrict.conf before overwriting.
